questions.question: In the rewritten markdownToHtml, why does the lexer run separately from the parser instead of calling md.parse directly?
choices: So top-level heading tokens can be tagged plansectionEligible before rendering, since the renderer alone cannot tell a top-level heading from one nested in a blockquote
Because marked's parse method does not support renderer overrides
To allow the highlighter to run asynchronously on code tokens
Because unflattenTables must run between lexing and parsing
questions.rationale: The explanation says the lexer runs separately so top-level heading tokens can be tagged plansectionEligible, which the renderer alone cannot determine.
questions.sourceSection: Rewrite markdownToHtml onto marked renderer overrides

questions.question: What desync problem did the old planSections line scan have that the marked-lexer rewrite fixes?
choices: Its naive ``` fence toggle could desync from the renderer on tilde (~~~) and indented fences, producing TOC anchors pointing at ids the body never emitted
It counted setext headings that the renderer ignored
It generated slugs with a different counter format like plan--a-1
It scanned headings of every depth instead of depths 2 through 4
questions.rationale: The old scan's naive fence toggle could disagree with the renderer on tilde and indented fences, causing TOC anchors and body ids to mismatch.
questions.sourceSection: Derive planSections from the marked lexer

questions.question: How does isSafeUrl defeat bypasses like a java\tscript: scheme?
choices: It normalizes away control characters that browsers strip before scheme parsing
It resolves the URL against the page origin before checking the scheme
It rejects any URL containing a backslash
It uses the URL constructor and catches parse exceptions
questions.rationale: isSafeUrl normalizes away control characters browsers strip before scheme parsing, so split schemes like java\tscript: are still caught.
questions.sourceSection: Block dangerous URL schemes in links and images

questions.question: Which helper survives the deletion of the hand-rolled inline and table renderers, and why?
choices: unflattenTables, because it still preprocesses one-line-flattened tables into proper multi-line input for marked's GFM table tokenizer
renderInline, because marked cannot handle code-span stashing
renderTable, because GFM tables need custom row splitting
isSeparatorRow, because marked has no separator-row detection
questions.rationale: unflattenTables remains as a string preprocessor so flattened tables reach marked's GFM table tokenizer as multi-line input, while the inline and table helpers are removed as dead code.
questions.sourceSection: Delete the hand-rolled inline and table renderers

questions.question: How is the marked library delivered to the plan status page?
choices: Vendored as a pinned UMD bundle at version 16.4.2, embedded in the Go server's embed.FS, and served from /assets/marked.min.js with no CDN or build step
Fetched at runtime from a CDN with a subresource integrity hash
Bundled into the page script by a new npm build step
Installed as a Go module dependency compiled into the server binary
questions.rationale: The library is vendored as a pinned marked@16.4.2 UMD bundle, added to embed.FS, and served from /assets/marked.min.js with no CDN or build step.
questions.sourceSection: Vendor, embed, and serve the marked asset
